landmarks.landmark_blurb: Made the Morgan Library a public institution in 1924 in accordance with his father's will.

grandchildren.n: Rear Admiral Henry Sturgis Morgan Jr.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Made the Morgan Library a public institution in 1924 in accordance with his father's will.

cross_connections.display_label: cousin of Edward Wigglesworth, who married this Rackemann

narrative_blocks.header: She managed the Morgan library and art collection
narrative_blocks.body: Known after her marriage as Mrs. J. P. Morgan Jr., Jane Norton Grew curated and managed the Morgan library and art collection and kept gardens at the family's Glen Cove estate on Long Island. She hosted royalty in both London and New York society.

narrative_blocks.header: He co-founded Morgan Stanley
narrative_blocks.body: When the Glass-Steagall Act of 1933 forced the separation of investment and commercial banking, Henry Sturgis Morgan and Harold Stanley left J.P. Morgan & Co. in 1935 and co-founded Morgan Stanley. In WWII, Morgan served as a Navy lieutenant commander, OSS agent, and Japanese translator. He served as president and chairman of the Morgan Library, commodore of the New York Yacht Club (like his father and grandfather before him), and was posthumously inducted into the America's Cup Hall of Fame in 2001.

narrative_blocks.header: He co-founded Morgan Stanley because Glass-Steagall forced
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Sturgis Morgan co-founded Morgan Stanley with Harold Stanley in 1935 when Glass-Steagall forced investment/commercial banking separation from J.P. Morgan & Co. In WWII he served with the OSS — predecessor of the CIA — as a Navy commander and Japanese translator. He served as Commodore of the New York Yacht Club, like his father and grandfather before him, and was posthumously inducted into the America's Cup Hall of Fame in 2001.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Served as trustee, president, and chairman of the Morgan Library & Museum.

institutions.institution_blurb: Harvard University class of 1923. Harvard Overseer 1933-1941.

cross_connections.display_label: His father-in-law was the 44th Secretary of the Navy, a great-great-grandson of President John Adams.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father was Secretary of the Navy
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine Lovering Adams was the daughter of Charles Francis Adams III, U.S. Secretary of the Navy under President Hoover, and a descendant of Presidents John Adams and John Quincy Adams.

narrative_blocks.header: He commanded submarines, a missile cruiser, then sat
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Morgan Jr. entered the Navy in 1942 at age 18, was commissioned from Harvard in 1944, and went on to command a submarine, a submarine division, a submarine tender, and a missile cruiser. He served as naval commander for the UN Command in Korea and as senior member of the U.S. delegation to the Korean armistice commission at Panmunjom. He retired as Rear Admiral in 1975.

narrative_blocks.header: He was still racing sailboats and coaching
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Morgan Jr. had begun sailing in 1930 at age six. He was still racing and coaching the Naval Academy Offshore Sailing Team up to his death in May 2011. His wife Sandy was granddaughter of Admiral John S.

bio.display_name: Rear Admiral Henry Sturgis Morgan Jr.
bio.title: Rear Admiral

notable.notable_blurb: devoted 40 years to preserving Anne Morgan's legacy

institutions.institution_blurb: Harvard University — B.A. History and Literature, class of 1950.

narrative_blocks.header: Conducted under Dean Dixon at Frankfurt Radio Orchestra
narrative_blocks.body: Miles Morgan studied conducting in Rome under Franco Ferrara at the Accademia Nazionale di Santa Cecilia after his Harvard graduation and Army service. He served as assistant conductor to Dean Dixon at the Frankfurt Radio Orchestra. He went on to become music director of the Bangor Symphony Orchestra for six years.

narrative_blocks.header: Janus Films officer brought Bergman to American audiences
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1960s Miles was an original officer and director of Janus Films — the company that introduced American audiences to Ingmar Bergman, Akira Kurosawa, and Jean-Luc Godard of the French New Wave. He was an international citizen who lived in Rome, Paris, Provence, Venice, Miami, and Manhattan across his career.

narrative_blocks.header: Forty years preserving great-aunt Anne Morgan's WWI legacy
narrative_blocks.body: Miles spent four decades championing the legacy of his great-aunt Anne Morgan, daughter of John Pierpont Morgan and organizer of WWI relief from the Château de Blérancourt in Picardy. He toured an 18-city exhibition on American women rebuilding France, secured a 2019 PBS broadcast of the film Anne Morgan's War, and served on the scientific committee when the Blérancourt museum was rebuilt in 2016. France honored him with the Légion d'Honneur and the rank of Officier in the Ordre des Arts et Lettres.

cross_connections.display_label: Great-aunt; Miles preserved her WWI legacy across forty years.

military_service.notes: U.S. Army 1951-1953 — Korean War era service. Specific unit, rank, and theater of service undocumented. Per existing research_notes on this entry. Son of Henry Sturgis Morgan (HD1175) of the J.P. Morgan banking dynasty.

burial.plot_notes: Cedar Hill Cemetery, Hartford CT — Morgan family plot. Same cemetery as his father Henry Sturgis Morgan (HD1175, 1900-1982 Morgan Stanley co-founder), grandfather John Pierpont Morgan II (HD1172, 1867-1943), and great-grandfather John Pierpont Morgan (HD1170, 1837-1913 of J.P. Morgan & Co.).

narrative_blocks.header: He won Olympic gold in sailing at Helsinki
narrative_blocks.body: John Adams Morgan competed at the 1952 Summer Olympics in Helsinki in the 6 Metre class, winning gold with the boat Llanoria as part of a six-person crew. He was 21 years old. He graduated from Yale the following year, in 1953.

narrative_blocks.header: He started Morgan Joseph because 'the pace is
narrative_blocks.body: After careers at Dominick & Dominick and Smith Barney (VP and vice chairman of M&A), Morgan told the New York Times in 1986: 'The physical and mental pressures of investment banking are tremendous. Here the atmosphere is more pleasant and the pace is survivable.' The Times noted that in banking 'pedigree opens doors' — his Morgan name built 'the network of relationships, alliances and directorships that has made his small house among the most profitable of the new investment boutiques.' He co-founded Morgan Joseph in 2002 and chaired it until 2016.

narrative_blocks.header: He owned a 3.5-acre private island off Stamford
narrative_blocks.body: John Adams Morgan owned Caritas Island, a 3.5-acre private island compound off the coast of Stamford, Connecticut, with a 26-room, 14,000-square-foot home originally built in 1906. He listed it for sale in 2011 for $18.9 million.

narrative_blocks.header: He served on the Morgan Library board
narrative_blocks.body: John Adams Morgan served on the board of directors of the Morgan Library & Museum (LMK040) for 35 years, until his death in January 2025. He was the great-grandson of J.P. Morgan, the Library's founder, and the son of Henry Sturgis Morgan, its longtime president and chairman.

institutions.institution_blurb: Groton School. Also attended Harvard College 1956-57, Adelphi College 1959-1961, and SUNY Farmingdale (A.A. automotive technology, 1963).

narrative_blocks.header: He worked on nuclear submarines
narrative_blocks.body: Peter Angus Morgan served in the Navy until 1970 as a mechanical operator and lab technician on nuclear submarines. He then spent his career at General Electric: twelve years with the heat transfer unit, then the cryogenic branch working with superconducting magnets. After retirement he dedicated much of his time to Habitat for Humanity.

notable.notable_blurb: Morgan Guaranty Trust director; J.P. Morgan grandson

military_service.notes: Served as Ensign in United States Naval Reserve Force (USNRF) during World War I, after graduating Harvard 1914.

narrative_blocks.header: Director of Morgan Guaranty Trust Company
narrative_blocks.body: Junius Spencer Morgan III served as director of Morgan Guaranty Trust Company — the successor institution to J.P. Morgan and Company that consolidated his family's banking interests across the twentieth century. The director role placed him in the third generation of working Morgans at the family bank, after his father J.P. Morgan Jr. (who succeeded his own father at the firm) and grandfather J. Pierpont Morgan Sr.

narrative_blocks.header: Ensign United States Naval Reserve, World War I
narrative_blocks.body: After graduating Harvard with an A.B. in 1914, Junius served as Ensign in the United States Naval Reserve Force during World War I — one of many Ivy League graduates of his class year who took commissions in the naval reserve as the United States entered the European war in 1917. The military service preceded his return to civilian banking work at the family firm.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Louise Converse 1915 with three children
narrative_blocks.body: Junius married Louise Converse in 1915 and they had three children together. The 1915 marriage came one year after his Harvard graduation and two years before his Naval Reserve commission. The Converse-Morgan family settled into the established New York and Long Island social circles of the post-Gilded-Age Morgan clan.

narrative_blocks.header: First cousin to Lost Generation poet Harry Crosby
narrative_blocks.body: Junius's first cousin was modernist poet Harry Crosby (1898-1929) — Lost Generation figure, Black Sun Press co-founder whose Paris-based press first published Hemingway, James Joyce, D.H. Lawrence, and Hart Crane. Harry died sensationally in a 1929 New York murder-suicide pact with his lover Josephine Noyes Rotch at age thirty-one. The first-cousin link via the Grew sisters — Junius's mother Jane Norton Grew married J.P. Morgan Jr., while Harry's mother Henrietta Grew married Boston banker Stephen Van Rensselaer Crosby — placed Junius's straight-laced Morgan banking world in stark contrast to Harry's expatriate-bohemian Paris.

narrative_blocks.header: Born NYC 1892, died Simcoe Ontario 1960
narrative_blocks.body: Junius Spencer Morgan III was born March 15, 1892 in New York City and died October 19, 1960 in Simcoe, Ontario Canada at age sixty-eight. The end-of-life Canadian-Ontario location was unusual for a working New York banker; the reasons for his time in Simcoe in his final years are not surfaced in available sources.

HD1170.prominent_name_reason: J.P. Morgan (1837-1913) — greatest financier of the Gilded Age; formed U.S. Steel, GE, International Harvester; controlled 21 railroads. His middle name "Pierpont" honors his mother Juliet Pierpont's descent from Rev. Thomas Hooker. 7th-generation Hooker descendant.

cross_connections.display_label: Roosevelt dissolved his Northern Securities Trust 1904
produced building designs for him as Gilded Age architect
amassed a Gilded Age art collection rivaling Morgan's

institutions.institution_blurb: Built his private library on East 36th Street to house his collection of medieval manuscripts, Old Master drawings, and rare books; opened to the public after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Personally bailed out the US Treasury — twice
narrative_blocks.body: During the 1895 gold crisis he led a syndicate that supplied the federal government with $65 million in gold to prevent default. During the Panic of 1907 he locked the heads of New York's banks in his library until they pledged the funds to halt a wave of failures.

narrative_blocks.header: Created the first billion-dollar corporation
narrative_blocks.body: In 1901 he merged Carnegie Steel with smaller producers to form U.S. Steel, capitalized at $1.4 billion when the entire federal budget was under $500 million. Andrew Carnegie reportedly named his price on a slip of paper, and Morgan replied 'I accept' without negotiation.

narrative_blocks.header: Disfiguring rhinophyma made him hate cameras
narrative_blocks.body: A skin condition called rhinophyma transformed his nose into a bulbous purple mass in middle age. He refused most photographs and detested social engagements; the few portraits that exist were heavily retouched on his orders.

narrative_blocks.header: Built one of the world's greatest art collections
narrative_blocks.body: He spent decades and a fortune assembling Renaissance manuscripts, Old Masters paintings, and ancient artifacts; at his death in 1913 his collection was valued near $50 million. Half of it was donated to the Metropolitan Museum of Art by his son and remains core to the Met today.

narrative_blocks.header: Pujo Committee testimony broke his health
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1912 he was forced to testify before the Pujo Committee investigating his 'money trust'; the Senate hearings exhausted him and he sailed for Europe to recover. He died in Rome on March 31, 1913, three months after taking the stand.
